questions.stem: Which auricular condition is acquired, not congenital?
questions.explanation: Othematoma (auricular haematoma) is traumatic/acquired. Microtia, preauricular fistula and tag are congenital.

questions.stem: Swelling of the supraclavicular lymph nodes is:

options.B: Usually due to metastasis from lung and GI carcinomas
options.C: The commonest finding in laryngeal cancer

questions.explanation: Supraclavicular nodes (Virchow) classically signal metastasis from thoraco-abdominal primaries — lung and GI.

questions.stem: Which thyroid carcinoma has the worst prognosis?
questions.explanation: Anaplastic — rapidly fatal in the elderly. Papillary has the best prognosis.

questions.stem: Complications of acute rhinosinusitis can be:
questions.explanation: All of the above — orbital (commonest), intracranial (meningitis/abscess, cavernous sinus thrombosis) and osseous (Pott's puffy tumour).

questions.stem: Which is NOT indicated in acute otitis media?

options.D: Washing of the external auditory canal

questions.explanation: Canal washing is for otitis externa/wax, not AOM (a middle-ear infection behind an intact drum).

questions.stem: How is cholesteatoma formed?

options.A: Retraction & perforation in the attic (epitympanic) part of the TM

questions.explanation: Acquired cholesteatoma starts as a retraction pocket/perforation of the pars flaccida (attic) trapping squamous epithelium.

questions.stem: Mechanical→electrical energy transformation in hearing occurs at:

options.D: The hair cells of the cochlea

questions.explanation: Transduction occurs at the hair cells of the organ of Corti.

questions.stem: The peritonsillar abscess:

options.A: Is treated only with IV antibiotics
options.B: Is a unilateral pus collection between tonsil and superior constrictor
options.C: Presents with severe dysphagia and trismus

questions.explanation: Both b and c — unilateral pus lateral to the tonsil, with trismus, odynophagia, muffled voice; needs drainage + antibiotics.

questions.stem: In sinus imaging, which single statement is correct?

options.A: Plain X-ray has been almost completely replaced by CT
options.D: MRI and ultrasound are the first choice

questions.explanation: CT has largely replaced plain films for sinuses (best for bone/ostia); MRI is reserved for soft tissue/intracranial extension.

questions.stem: Voice restoration after total laryngectomy can use:
questions.explanation: All three are valid rehabilitation options.

questions.stem: Blood supply of the palatine tonsil is from:

options.A: Branches of the external carotid artery
options.B: Branches of the internal carotid artery

questions.explanation: Tonsillar branches arise from the external carotid system (facial, ascending pharyngeal, lingual, lesser palatine).

questions.stem: Which skin cancer of the external nose can metastasize to regional nodes?
questions.explanation: SCC can metastasize to nodes; BCC almost never does.

questions.stem: Regarding oropharyngeal cancer, which is NOT true?

options.A: Related to tobacco/alcohol and Epstein-Barr virus
options.B: Can be asymptomatic for a long time
options.C: May present with foreign-body sensation, dysphagia, otalgia
options.D: Treated with surgery, RT, chemo or combinations

questions.explanation: Oropharyngeal SCC is linked to HPV (+ tobacco/alcohol). EBV is the nasopharyngeal association — so that statement is false.

questions.stem: Tonsillectomy indications do NOT include:
questions.explanation: Snoring alone isn't an indication; obstructive sleep apnoea/airway obstruction is.

questions.stem: Which complication is NOT associated with middle-ear infection?
questions.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombophlebitis complicates sinusitis/facial infection. Otitis media gives sigmoid sinus thrombosis.

questions.stem: All of the following are ototoxic EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Cephalosporins are not ototoxic. Aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, salicylates and platinum chemo are.

questions.stem: A child 3–6 years with conductive hearing loss most likely has:
questions.explanation: Secretory otitis media (glue ear) — the commonest cause of conductive loss in young children.

questions.stem: For nasal polyps, all are true EXCEPT:

options.A: They are a cause of severe epistaxis
options.C: Associated with asthma, allergy, aspirin intolerance
options.D: Steroid sprays are a mainstay of treatment

questions.explanation: Polyps are pale and do not bleed significantly. Severe epistaxis in a young male suggests juvenile angiofibroma.

questions.stem: The nasal septum consists of:

options.D: Quadrilateral cartilage + vomer + perpendicular plate of ethmoid

questions.explanation: Septum = septal (quadrilateral) cartilage + vomer + perpendicular plate of the ethmoid.

questions.stem: Drug indicated for idiopathic Bell's palsy:
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids (± antivirals) are first-line for Bell's palsy.

questions.stem: In nasal fractures, all are true EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Nasal fractures do not always need immediate surgery — reduce within ~2 weeks. Drain a septal haematoma urgently.

questions.stem: Labyrinthine concussion is:

options.A: Impact of the membranous labyrinth against its bony capsule
options.B: Exclusively due to petrous bone fracture
options.D: A cause of facial nerve paralysis

questions.explanation: It is the membranous labyrinth striking the bony walls — no fracture required.

questions.stem: Which is true about cervical swellings?

options.B: Adults over 40 should be considered benign
options.C: Night sweats + weight loss raise suspicion of malignancy
options.D: Painful swellings are always acute inflammation

questions.explanation: Night sweats and weight loss are red flags (lymphoma) → investigate. Over-40 masses must NOT be assumed benign.

questions.stem: Zenker's diverticulum is located in:
questions.explanation: A pharyngeal (hypopharyngeal) pouch through Killian's dehiscence → dysphagia, regurgitation, halitosis.

questions.stem: Barotrauma is caused by:
questions.explanation: Pressure changes the Eustachian tube can't equalise (flying/diving) → middle-ear barotrauma.

questions.stem: Trismus is:
questions.explanation: Trismus = restricted mouth opening (e.g. quinsy, parapharyngeal abscess, Ludwig's, TMJ).

questions.stem: The most common site of epistaxis is:
questions.explanation: Over 90% arise from Little's area / Kiesselbach plexus on the anterior septum. (The exam statement "middle concha" was the wrong one.)

questions.stem: Cancer of the lower lip is associated with:
questions.explanation: Sun exposure + pipe smoking (+ age) are the classic risk factors for lower-lip SCC.

questions.stem: In functional voice disorders, all are true EXCEPT:

options.B: Treatment of hyperfunctional dysphonia is primarily surgical
options.C: Boys may keep a high-pitched voice

questions.explanation: Hyperfunctional/functional dysphonia is treated with voice therapy, not surgery.

questions.stem: Which finding confirms labyrinthitis?

options.B: Rotational vertigo and sensorineural hearing loss

questions.explanation: Labyrinthitis = vertigo + SNHL (± tinnitus). Vestibular neuritis is the same but without hearing loss.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a complication of acute tonsillitis?
questions.explanation: Membranous tonsillitis is a type/presentation, not a complication. Complications: quinsy, rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis, sepsis.

questions.stem: In children, laryngeal oedema is most dangerous in the:
questions.explanation: The subglottis is the narrowest part of a child's airway → small oedema causes big obstruction (e.g. croup).

questions.stem: Which condition is characterized by watery nasal discharge?
questions.explanation: CSF rhinorrhoea = clear watery discharge; confirm with beta-2 transferrin.

questions.stem: The nasal cycle alternates patency every:
questions.explanation: The physiological nasal cycle alternates congestion between sides roughly every 2–5 hours.

questions.stem: Which sinus does NOT drain into the middle meatus?
questions.explanation: Posterior ethmoid cells drain into the superior meatus. Frontal, maxillary and anterior ethmoid drain to the middle meatus.

questions.stem: Which is INCORRECT about otosclerosis?

options.A: Affects women more, accelerated by pregnancy
options.B: Conductive loss from stapes footplate fixation
options.D: Main symptoms are SNHL, tinnitus and vertigo

questions.explanation: Otosclerosis causes conductive hearing loss (stapes fixation). "Main symptoms SNHL/vertigo" is the false statement.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true in the work-up of cervical swellings?

options.A: On FNA cytology mainly negative results are evaluated to exclude malignancy
options.B: Ultrasound shows position, size, solid/cystic nature
options.C: CT shows relation to vessels and infiltration
options.D: Open biopsy is most reliable but done last

questions.explanation: FNA cytology is interpreted on positive diagnostic findings; a negative FNA does not exclude malignancy.

questions.stem: Frey's (auriculotemporal) syndrome follows:
questions.explanation: Parotidectomy → aberrant parasympathetic reinnervation of sweat glands → gustatory sweating (Frey's).

questions.stem: Nystagmus:

options.B: Occurs only in irritative vestibular lesions
options.C: Is a sensation of eye fatigue

questions.explanation: Vertical nystagmus = central lesion. Peripheral nystagmus is horizontal/rotatory and suppressed by fixation.

questions.stem: Which is true for choanal atresia?

options.C: It always occurs within a syndrome

questions.explanation: About 90% bony, 10% membranous. Bilateral cases present as a neonatal emergency; associated with CHARGE.

questions.stem: Nasal polyps in childhood are associated with:
questions.explanation: Always consider cystic fibrosis in a child with nasal polyps.

questions.stem: A patient with acute tonsillitis presents with all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Referred otalgia occurs, but hearing loss is not a feature of acute tonsillitis.

questions.stem: Macroglossia is seen in:
questions.explanation: Down syndrome and acromegaly (also amyloidosis, hypothyroidism).

questions.stem: Which is true for nasal polyps?

options.D: They arise from the floor of the nose

questions.explanation: FESS ± steroids; they frequently recur, are benign, and arise around the middle meatus.

questions.stem: Vocal cord nodules — only one is true:
questions.explanation: Nodules are bilateral (voice abuse), benign, managed with voice therapy; they don't cause dyspnoea.

questions.stem: For subglottic stenosis, all apply EXCEPT:

options.A: MRI has a role in diagnosis
options.B: Prolonged intubation is a common cause
options.D: Tracheostomy technique relates to stenosis risk

questions.explanation: MRI has no real role — diagnosis is by endoscopy/CT.

questions.stem: Treatment of acute otitis externa:
questions.explanation: Analgesia + topical antibiotic/steroid drops + aural toilet. Systemic antibiotics only if spreading.

questions.stem: Malignant neoplasms of the oral cavity are usually:
questions.explanation: Squamous cell carcinoma; main risk factors smoking + alcohol (+ HPV, sun for lip).

questions.stem: Juvenile angiofibroma:

options.A: Presents with severe epistaxis + nasal obstruction

questions.explanation: Adolescent males, very vascular, benign but locally aggressive; severe epistaxis + obstruction; biopsy avoided.

questions.stem: For nasal foreign bodies, which is true?

options.B: After removal, check for a second foreign body

questions.explanation: Always re-examine for a second FB. They affect young children and cause unilateral foul discharge.

questions.stem: All are causes of vocal cord paralysis EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Lower-limb surgery doesn't threaten the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Thyroid/neck surgery, idiopathic and cervical spine do.

questions.stem: In superior laryngeal nerve palsy there is:

options.B: Cricothyroid palsy, altered vocal range, difficulty singing

questions.explanation: The external branch supplies cricothyroid (the pitch/tensor muscle) → loss of high pitch, trouble singing.

questions.stem: A patient with reduced saliva after head-and-neck radiotherapy may have:
questions.explanation: Radiation xerostomia → caries, candidiasis, dysgeusia — all of the above.

questions.stem: Which statement is INCORRECT?

options.A: Mumps is usually unilateral with low fever
options.B: Mumps may cause sensorineural hearing loss
options.C: Acute suppurative sialadenitis is usually Staph aureus
options.D: Chronic recurrent sialadenitis occurs in children, cause unknown

questions.explanation: Mumps is usually bilateral — that statement is false. It can cause SNHL; suppurative sialadenitis = S. aureus.

questions.stem: The submandibular gland duct opens at:

options.A: The vestibule near the lower-lip frenulum
options.B: The salivary papilla in the vestibule

questions.explanation: Wharton's duct opens at the sublingual caruncle (floor of mouth, beside the lingual frenulum).

questions.stem: Which is true about cervical swellings?

options.A: Supraclavicular swellings are usually lipomas/branchial cysts
options.B: Parotid swellings are usually malignant with VII palsy
options.C: Posterior-triangle multiple small mobile nodes → TB or NPC metastasis
options.D: Dermoid cysts move with both swallowing and tongue protrusion

questions.explanation: Multiple small mobile posterior-triangle nodes suggest TB or nasopharyngeal carcinoma metastasis. (Dermoids move with swallowing but NOT tongue protrusion — that's the thyroglossal cyst.)

questions.stem: Regarding a ranula, which is true?

options.C: It protrudes on the inner cheek
options.D: It results from blocked sebaceous glands

questions.explanation: A ranula is a saliva-retention (mucous) cyst in the floor of mouth (sublingual gland); treat by marsupialisation/excision.

questions.stem: The precancerous lesions of the oral cavity are:
questions.explanation: Leukoplakia + erythroplakia (erythroplakia has the higher malignant potential).

questions.stem: Herpetic gingivostomatitis is caused by:
questions.explanation: HSV-1.

questions.stem: OSA investigation includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Brain CT isn't part of the OSA work-up; polysomnography + airway endoscopy/Mueller are.

questions.stem: Oesophageal foreign bodies in children are usually found at:
questions.explanation: The first (cricopharyngeal) narrowing is the commonest site of impaction.

questions.stem: After total thyroidectomy, bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causes mainly:
questions.explanation: Bilateral RLN palsy → both cords paramedian → inspiratory stridor / airway obstruction (may need tracheostomy).

questions.stem: Which auricular condition is acquired, not congenital?
questions.explanation: Perichondritis is an acquired infection; the others are congenital.

questions.stem: In chronic otitis media, which is NOT seen?
questions.explanation: Perichondritis is an auricular cartilage problem, not a feature of chronic OM.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a cause of epistaxis?
questions.explanation: Vasomotor rhinitis is not a classic epistaxis cause; bleeding disorders, HHT and anticoagulation are.

questions.stem: Which ocular complication of sinusitis needs surgery?
questions.explanation: Orbital cellulitis / subperiosteal abscess needs urgent drainage; preseptal signs are managed medically.

questions.stem: Which is NOT part of nasal polyp treatment?
questions.explanation: Bronchodilators treat the airway, not the polyps. Steroids + FESS (± antihistamines for allergy) are used.

questions.stem: Acute rhinosinusitis most commonly affects:

options.A: Ethmoid in children, maxillary in adults
options.B: Frontal in children, maxillary in adults

questions.explanation: Ethmoid sinusitis predominates in children; maxillary in adults.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true regarding secretory otitis media?
questions.explanation: Glue ear shows a type B (flat) tympanogram, not type A.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true of acute acoustic trauma?

options.A: May be caused by a blast/firearm
options.B: Initially presents with bleeding from the ear canal

questions.explanation: Acoustic trauma causes tinnitus + SNHL, not canal bleeding.

questions.stem: In BPPV, which is NOT true?

options.A: Drug therapy is the treatment of choice

questions.explanation: BPPV is treated with repositioning manoeuvres (Epley), not drugs.

questions.stem: Acute otitis media usually results from:
questions.explanation: AOM follows a URTI ascending via the Eustachian tube.

questions.stem: In voice rehabilitation after laryngectomy:

options.A: Oesophageal speech is easy to learn
options.C: Voice prostheses sit in a tracheo-oesophageal fistula

questions.explanation: Tracheo-oesophageal voice prostheses are placed in a surgically created fistula.

questions.stem: The skeleton of the larynx — best answer among the options:
questions.explanation: Anatomically the larynx has 9 cartilages (3 unpaired: thyroid, cricoid, epiglottis + 3 paired: arytenoid, corniculate, cuneiform) with the hyoid bone above. None of the options is fully correct; "5 cartilages + 1 bone" is the closest framing (main cartilages + hyoid). Flagged as an ambiguous source question.

questions.stem: In emergency airway management, all are true EXCEPT:

options.B: Tracheostomy can be a permanent airway
options.D: O₂ + nebulised adrenaline are conservative measures

questions.explanation: Cricothyroidotomy/coniotomy is an emergency, TEMPORARY airway, converted to a formal tracheostomy later.

questions.stem: Most common cause of unilateral nasal obstruction with foul-smelling mucosanguineous discharge in a child:
questions.explanation: Unilateral obstruction + foul, blood-tinged discharge in a child = a nasal foreign body until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: Diagnosis of a tracheobronchial foreign body is based on:

options.A: A positive history (paroxysmal cough, choking, cyanosis)

questions.explanation: All of the above — history is key (a witnessed choking episode), supported by auscultation and imaging.

questions.stem: Regarding the external auditory canal:

options.B: The skin of the cartilaginous portion is thin and sensitive
options.D: The bony-portion skin has hairs and ceruminous glands

questions.explanation: The outer third is cartilaginous (thick skin with hair + ceruminous glands); the inner two-thirds is bony (thin, sensitive skin, no glands).

questions.stem: Which is INCORRECT about hearing-assessment methods?

options.A: Otoacoustic emissions are high-frequency sounds produced in the brainstem
options.B: Evoked potentials differentiate cochlear from retrocochlear lesions
options.D: OAEs are used in neonatal screening

questions.explanation: OAEs are low-intensity sounds generated by the cochlear outer hair cells, not the brainstem — that statement is false.

questions.stem: In the Romberg (standing) test of labyrinthine function:

options.A: The patient always falls toward the lesion side
options.B: Falls toward the lesion side if the lesion is irritative
options.C: Falls toward the lesion side if the lesion is paralytic

questions.explanation: With a paralytic/destructive peripheral lesion the patient falls toward the affected side (toward the slow-phase direction).

questions.stem: Sialolithiasis:

options.A: Is commoner in the parotid than the submandibular gland
options.D: May cause inflammation and pus from the duct orifice

questions.explanation: Stones (80–90% submandibular) cause painful mealtime swelling and can lead to infection with pus at the duct opening.

questions.stem: Which does NOT contribute to swallowing?

options.A: Cranial nerves V, VII, IX, X, XII

questions.explanation: CN III and VI move the eyes — they have no role in swallowing. Swallowing uses V, VII, IX, X, XII.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a manifestation of peripheral facial-nerve palsy?
questions.explanation: VII palsy → taste loss (chorda tympani), dry eye (greater petrosal), hyperacusis (stapedius). Epistaxis is unrelated.

questions.stem: Which symptom is NOT characteristic of Ménière’s disease?
questions.explanation: Ménière’s = vertigo + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness. Headache is not part of it.

questions.stem: The prognosis of laryngeal cancer depends on:
questions.explanation: All of the above — site, stage and nodal status all drive prognosis.

questions.stem: Acute laryngitis:

options.C: May present with hoarseness, pain on speech/swallow, cough, dyspnoea
options.D: Is always treated with IV antibiotics

questions.explanation: Usually self-limiting (often viral); presents with hoarseness, sore throat, cough ± mild dyspnoea — not always urgent or needing IV antibiotics.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true regarding laryngeal neoplasms?

options.A: The commonest malignancy is squamous cell carcinoma
options.B: Laryngeal neoplasms always present with hoarseness
options.C: Advanced tumours may cause dyspnoea or haemoptysis
options.D: Supraglottic tumours metastasise to nodes more often

questions.explanation: Not always hoarseness — supraglottic/subglottic tumours can be silent until advanced. Glottic ones cause early hoarseness.

questions.stem: Laryngeal injuries lead to:
questions.explanation: All of the above.

questions.stem: Othematoma is:

options.B: A collection of blood between cartilage and perichondrium on the anterior auricle
options.C: A traumatic blood collection in the middle ear

questions.explanation: Sub-perichondrial haematoma on the anterior surface — must be drained to prevent "cauliflower ear".

questions.stem: The maxillary sinus drains into the:
questions.explanation: Middle meatus (its ostium sits high → poor gravity drainage → prone to sinusitis).

questions.stem: All may cause cervical swellings EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: A laryngeal polyp sits on the vocal cord and does not produce a neck mass.

questions.stem: In sensorineural hearing loss, which is correct?

options.A: Not curable but aided with hearing aids
options.C: Aided with hearing aids if ≤80–90 dB

questions.explanation: SNHL is not curable, aided if ≤80–90 dB, and harms childhood speech. It does NOT respond to long-term drugs, so "all of the above" is wrong — the correct combo is statements 1 + 3 + 4.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true about salivary gland tumours?

options.B: Most salivary tumours are in the parotid

questions.explanation: Main treatment is surgery, not chemotherapy.

questions.stem: During the Rinne test:

options.A: A 512 Hz fork is placed on the forehead
options.B: In normal/SNHL ears air conduction is heard better
options.C: In conductive loss air and bone are heard equally
options.D: In normal/SNHL ears bone conduction is heard better

questions.explanation: Normal hearing or SNHL → air conduction > bone (Rinne positive). (Forehead placement is the Weber test.)

questions.stem: Seasonal allergic rhinitis presents with:
questions.explanation: All of the above.

questions.stem: Investigation of sleep apnoea includes:

options.D: Flexible scope + Mueller manoeuvre AND a sleep study

questions.explanation: Airway endoscopy + Mueller manoeuvre together with polysomnography (sleep study). Brain CT has no role.

questions.stem: The tympanic membrane:

options.A: Has a pars tensa and a "perforated" part
options.B: On otoscopy the crura of the stapes are seen
options.C: Separates the external from the middle ear
options.D: Is part of the inner ear

questions.explanation: It separates external and middle ear. Two parts = pars tensa + pars flaccida; the malleus (not stapes) is seen; it is not inner ear.

questions.stem: Congenital causes of nasal obstruction include:
questions.explanation: All of the above.

questions.stem: Indications for tracheotomy:

options.A: Bypass of obstruction above the trachea
options.C: Prevent tracheal stenosis in prolonged intubation
options.D: Bypass of obstruction above the trachea AND prevention of stenosis in prolonged intubation

questions.explanation: Bypass an upper-airway obstruction + protect against stenosis in prolonged intubation.

questions.stem: In BPPV, all are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Diagnosed with a specific head-positioning test
options.D: Rotational vertigo on lying down/getting up

questions.explanation: BPPV is not confined to the elderly; it can occur at any adult age.

questions.stem: A parotid swelling present for >10 years now grows and becomes painful. Most likely:
questions.explanation: A long-standing pleomorphic adenoma that suddenly grows/pains suggests carcinoma ex-pleomorphic adenoma.

questions.stem: The commonest malignant tumour of the external nose is:
questions.explanation: Basal cell carcinoma is the commonest skin cancer of the face/nose (rarely metastasises).

questions.stem: A pharyngeal (nasopharyngeal) tumour may present with all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Nasopharyngeal tumours cause conductive hearing loss (serous otitis media via Eustachian-tube block), not SNHL.

questions.stem: A child 3–6 years with conductive hearing loss most likely has:
questions.explanation: Otitis media with effusion (glue ear) — the commonest cause of conductive loss in this age group.

questions.stem: In BPPV, which is NOT true?

options.A: The treatment of choice is medication
options.B: The treatment of choice is specific manoeuvres

questions.explanation: BPPV is treated with repositioning manoeuvres (Epley), not drugs.

questions.stem: Middle-ear infections may cause complications from the:
questions.explanation: Intracranial spread is into the middle cranial fossa (temporal-lobe abscess, meningitis). Cavernous-sinus thrombosis is a sinusitis complication.

questions.stem: Regarding laryngeal-cancer prognosis, all are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Cervical metastases do NOT significantly reduce survival
options.C: RT and laser cordectomy have similar T1 success
options.D: Glottic carcinoma has a better prognosis

questions.explanation: Nodal metastases markedly reduce survival — that statement is false. Glottic cancer genuinely has the best prognosis.

questions.stem: Indirect laryngoscopy shows the left vocal cord fixed in full abduction. This indicates:

options.A: Ipsilateral superior + recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy (complete vagal)
options.B: Ipsilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy only
options.C: Ipsilateral superior laryngeal nerve palsy only

questions.explanation: A cord in the intermediate/cadaveric (fully abducted) position = combined SLN + RLN (complete vagal) palsy. Isolated RLN palsy leaves the cord paramedian.

questions.stem: All of the following drugs are ototoxic EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Cephalosporins are not ototoxic. Aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, platinum chemo and salicylates are.

questions.stem: During the Rinne test:

options.A: A 512 Hz fork is placed on the forehead
options.B: In normal/SNHL ears air conduction is better
options.C: In normal/SNHL ears bone conduction is better
options.D: In conductive loss air and bone are equal

questions.explanation: Air conduction > bone in normal hearing and SNHL (Rinne positive).

questions.stem: Which drug is indicated for facial-nerve palsy?
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids (± antivirals) for Bell’s palsy.

questions.stem: Vocal cord granulomas:

options.A: Result from trauma/reflux, recur, and are premalignant
options.B: Affect the anterior third; treated with antacids, trauma avoidance, surgery if persistent
options.C: Result from trauma/reflux and are treated only surgically
options.D: Affect the posterior third; treated with antacids, trauma avoidance, surgery if persistent

questions.explanation: Granulomas form on the posterior third (vocal process) from reflux/trauma/intubation; managed with anti-reflux + voice rest, surgery only if persistent. Not premalignant.

questions.stem: Erysipelas of the auricle is caused by:
questions.explanation: Streptococcus.

questions.stem: Sudden mildly painful submandibular swelling that worsens with meals then subsides — most likely:

options.C: Obstruction — sialolithiasis of the submandibular gland

questions.explanation: Classic mealtime syndrome of sialolithiasis (Wharton’s duct obstruction).

questions.stem: Which is true of congenital cervical swellings?

options.A: Thyroglossal cyst lies anywhere between foramen cecum and the sternal notch
options.B: Cystic hygroma appears in early adulthood as a unilocular mass
options.C: Both lymphangiomas and haemangiomas regress spontaneously
options.D: 1st branchial fistulas open into the tonsillar fossa, 2nd into the pyriform sinus

questions.explanation: The thyroglossal duct runs from the foramen cecum of the tongue to the sternal notch, so the cyst can occur anywhere along it (usually near the hyoid).

questions.stem: Regarding nasopharyngeal carcinoma, which is NOT true?

options.A: A painless cervical node is often the first symptom
options.B: Diagnosis is usually made at an early stage
options.C: Unilateral serous otitis media is a common symptom
options.D: Cranial nerves III, IV, VI may be affected

questions.explanation: NPC is usually diagnosed late (hidden in the fossa of Rosenmüller) — that statement is false.

questions.stem: In a temporal-bone fracture through the middle ear, you may get:
questions.explanation: A (longitudinal) fracture through the middle ear → ossicular disruption / haemotympanum → conductive hearing loss.

questions.stem: The tympanic membrane:

options.A: Shows the stapes crura on otoscopy
options.B: Separates the external from the middle ear
options.C: Has a pars tensa and a "perforated" part
options.D: Is part of the inner ear

questions.explanation: It separates external and middle ear.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding tracheobronchial foreign bodies?

options.A: As the FB moves into segmental bronchi, dyspnoea worsens
options.B: They usually affect school-age children and adults
options.C: Differential dx includes laryngeal oedema, laryngotracheitis, atelectasis-pneumonia, bronchiectasis
options.D: Aspiration usually occurs when a toddler reacts suddenly with food in the mouth

questions.explanation: Aspiration typically happens during a toddler’s sudden movement/cry with food in the mouth. Flagged: option C (the differential-diagnosis list) is also a true statement, so this source item has two defensible answers.

questions.stem: Regarding oropharyngeal carcinoma, which is TRUE?

options.A: Rarely has nodal metastasis at diagnosis
options.B: Linked to tobacco abuse and Epstein-Barr virus
options.D: Linked to alcohol abuse and HPV

questions.explanation: Oropharyngeal SCC is linked to alcohol + tobacco + HPV (HPV, not EBV — EBV is nasopharyngeal). It commonly presents with neck nodes.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true in the work-up of cervical swellings?

options.A: On FNA cytology mainly negative results are evaluated to exclude malignancy
options.B: Ultrasound gives location, size, solid/cystic nature
options.C: CT shows relation to vessels and infiltration
options.D: Open biopsy is most reliable but done last

questions.explanation: FNA cytology is judged on positive diagnostic findings; a negative result does not exclude malignancy.

questions.stem: Which condition gives a watery nasal discharge?
questions.explanation: CSF rhinorrhoea — clear watery fluid; confirm with beta-2 transferrin.

questions.stem: Vocal cord granulomas:

options.A: Result from trauma/reflux, recur, are premalignant
options.B: Affect the posterior third; treated with anti-reflux, trauma avoidance, surgery if persistent
options.C: Affect the anterior third; treated medically, surgery if persistent
options.D: Result from trauma/reflux and are only treated surgically

questions.explanation: Posterior third (vocal process), reflux/trauma related; managed with anti-reflux + voice rest; not premalignant.

questions.stem: Which is true about the diagnostic approach to cervical swellings?

options.A: Under 20, lymphoma is the commonest cause
options.B: Over-40 masses should be considered benign
options.D: Painful swellings are always acute inflammation
options.E: Evening fever + weight loss raise suspicion and need work-up

questions.explanation: Evening (night) fever + weight loss are red flags for malignancy/lymphoma and must be investigated. Over-40 masses are NOT assumed benign.

questions.stem: What is the name of the largest anterior ethmoidal air cell?
questions.explanation: The ethmoidal bulla is the largest, most constant anterior ethmoid air cell (key osteomeatal-complex landmark).

questions.stem: The main symptom of Reinke’s oedema is:
questions.explanation: Reinke’s oedema (smoker’s polypoid corditis) → a deep, hoarse voice = dysphonia.

questions.stem: Which does NOT belong to the supraglottic larynx?
questions.explanation: The pyriform fossae are part of the hypopharynx, not the supraglottis (epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, false cords).

questions.stem: Regarding laryngeal-cancer prognosis, all are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Cervical metastases do NOT significantly reduce survival
options.C: RT and laser cordectomy have similar T1 success
options.D: Glottic carcinoma has a better prognosis

questions.explanation: Nodal metastases markedly worsen survival — false statement. Glottic cancer does have the best prognosis.

questions.stem: Othematoma is:

options.A: A traumatic blood collection in the middle ear
options.B: Blood between cartilage and perichondrium on the anterior auricle

questions.explanation: Sub-perichondrial haematoma on the anterior auricle — drain it to prevent cauliflower ear.

questions.stem: In sensorineural hearing loss:

options.B: Aided with hearing aids if ≤80–90 dB

questions.explanation: Aids help if ≤80–90 dB and childhood SNHL harms speech, but it does not respond to long-term drugs — so the answer is "2 + 3 only".

questions.stem: Erysipelas of the auricle is caused by:
questions.explanation: Streptococcus.

questions.stem: All of the following drugs are ototoxic EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Cephalosporins are not ototoxic.

questions.stem: During the Rinne test:

options.A: A 512 Hz fork is placed on the forehead
options.B: In normal/SNHL ears air conduction is better
options.C: In normal/SNHL ears bone conduction is better
options.D: In conductive loss air and bone are heard equally

questions.explanation: Air > bone in normal hearing and SNHL (Rinne positive).

questions.stem: The nasal cavity contains:
questions.explanation: The nasal valve (narrowest part of the airway, between the upper lateral cartilage and the septum).

questions.stem: Which statement is correct?

options.A: Taste from the tongue is carried by the chorda tympani
options.B: Motor supply to the tongue is the glossopharyngeal nerve
options.C: Hypoglossal injury reduces sensation of the anterior 2/3
options.D: Motor supply to the lips is the maxillary + mandibular nerves

questions.explanation: Chorda tympani (VII) carries taste from the anterior 2/3. Tongue motor = hypoglossal (XII); lip motor = facial (VII).

questions.stem: Which complication is NOT related to middle-ear infection?
questions.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombophlebitis complicates sinusitis/facial infection; OM causes sigmoid sinus thrombosis.

questions.stem: Nystagmus:

options.A: Is a sensation of eye fatigue in balance disorders
options.B: Is rhythmic eye movement accompanying SNHL
options.C: If vertical, indicates a central (CNS) lesion
options.D: Occurs only in irritative vestibular lesions

questions.explanation: Vertical nystagmus = central lesion.

questions.stem: How is a cholesteatoma formed?

options.A: By retraction and perforation of the TM in the epitympanic (attic) space

questions.explanation: A retraction pocket/perforation of the pars flaccida (attic) traps keratinising squamous epithelium.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding tracheobronchial foreign bodies?

options.A: Dried nuts are inert as they only partially obstruct
options.B: Radiopaque FBs are commoner than radiolucent ones
options.C: The commonest radiological finding is parenchymal consolidation
options.D: Avoiding nuts/small toy parts in under-3s + parent education reduces incidence

questions.explanation: Prevention (no nuts/small parts under 3 + education) reduces incidence. Most aspirated objects are organic/radiolucent; the usual film shows air-trapping, not consolidation.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a contraindication to tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy?
questions.explanation: Age under 7 is not a contraindication. Bleeding disorders, cleft palate and velopharyngeal insufficiency are (risk of bleeding / hypernasal speech).

questions.stem: Peritonsillar abscess:

options.A: Is treated only with IV antibiotics
options.B: Is a pus collection within the tonsil
options.C: Presents with severe dysphagia and trismus

questions.explanation: Quinsy → severe odynophagia + trismus (and a muffled "hot-potato" voice). Pus lies outside the tonsil (peritonsillar space) and needs drainage, not antibiotics alone.

questions.stem: Hypertrophy of the pharyngeal tonsil (adenoids) is NOT associated with:
questions.explanation: Adenoid hypertrophy causes conductive loss via Eustachian-tube obstruction/glue ear — not SNHL.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true in infectious mononucleosis?

options.C: It is caused by Epstein-Barr virus

questions.explanation: Mono shows a membranous/exudative (grey) tonsillitis, not a follicular pattern. It is EBV with atypical lymphocytosis and generalised nodes.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true regarding chronic pharyngitis?

options.A: May be due to permanent mouth breathing
options.D: May be due to chronic nasal inflammation

questions.explanation: Chronic pharyngitis is managed by removing irritants (smoking, mouth breathing, postnasal drip) — not long-term antibiotics.

questions.stem: Deviated nasal septum:

options.A: Is caused by pressure from nasal polyps
options.C: Is treated with the Caldwell-Luc procedure
options.D: Is common and, if severe, obstructs nasal breathing

questions.explanation: Septal deviation is common and obstructs breathing when severe (treated by septoplasty). Caldwell-Luc is a maxillary-sinus operation.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a test for the peripheral vestibular system?
questions.explanation: The tuning-fork (fork) test assesses hearing, not the vestibular system. Fistula, rotation and caloric tests are all vestibular.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a complication of middle-ear disease?
questions.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombosis complicates sinusitis/facial infection. Middle-ear disease causes sigmoid sinus thrombosis, mastoiditis, labyrinthitis and meningitis.

questions.stem: Which is a LOCAL cause of epistaxis?
questions.explanation: Trauma is local. Anticoagulants, bleeding disorders and hypertension are systemic (general) causes.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a cause of a white tongue?
questions.explanation: Fissured tongue = grooves, not a white plaque. Leukoplakia, oral lichen planus and candidiasis all produce white lesions.

questions.stem: Which is NOT an infectious cause of a neck lump?
questions.explanation: Vasomotor rhinitis is non-infectious (and not a neck lump). TB, mumps and HIV are infective causes of cervical swelling.

questions.stem: Which does NOT lead to septal perforation?
questions.explanation: Vasomotor rhinitis doesn’t perforate the septum. Trauma/surgery, cocaine, and Wegener’s (GPA) classically do (also infection, steroid overuse).

questions.stem: Which artery does NOT supply the internal nose?
questions.explanation: The ascending pharyngeal artery supplies the pharynx/tonsil, not the internal nose. The nose is fed by the ethmoidals (from the ophthalmic/ICA) and the sphenopalatine (+ greater palatine, superior labial) from the maxillary/ECA.

questions.stem: Which sinus drains into the superior nasal meatus?
questions.explanation: By elimination: frontal, maxillary and anterior ethmoid all drain to the middle meatus, so the answer here is sphenoid (it drains highest, via the sphenoethmoidal recess). Note: strictly, the posterior ethmoid cells are the true “superior-meatus” sinus — but they’re not offered as an option.

questions.stem: Which hearing test OBJECTIVELY measures the hearing threshold?
questions.explanation: ABR/BERA objectively measures threshold. PTA is subjective; OAE tests outer-hair-cell function; stapedius reflex is objective but not a threshold test.

questions.stem: Squamous cell carcinoma of the mouth most likely arises on the:
questions.explanation: The tongue (lateral/ventral border) and floor of mouth are the highest-risk intra-oral SCC sites.

questions.stem: Which does NOT cause a PAINFUL parotid swelling?
questions.explanation: A neoplasm (esp. benign pleomorphic adenoma) is classically a painless lump — pain is a red flag for malignancy. Mumps and sialolithiasis are painful. Flagged: sarcoidosis (Heerfordt) is also usually painless, so this source item is somewhat ambiguous — "neoplasia" is the expected key.

questions.stem: Allergic rhinitis — choose the WRONG statement:

options.A: Presents with a positive allergy test
options.D: Treated with decongestants, corticosteroids, oral antihistamines

questions.explanation: "Affects 40%" is overstated — allergic rhinitis prevalence is usually quoted ~10–25%. The other three statements are correct. (Mild ambiguity, as some regions report higher figures.)

questions.stem: Which structure is NOT adjacent to the sphenoid sinus?
questions.explanation: The mandibular nerve (V3) exits via foramen ovale into the infratemporal fossa — not related to the sphenoid sinus. The ICA, CN III (cavernous sinus wall) and V2 are all adjacent (plus optic nerve, cavernous sinus, Vidian nerve).

questions.stem: Which is NOT true of trigeminal neuralgia?

options.A: Ipsilateral lacrimation and rhinorrhoea are common
options.C: The ophthalmic division is most often affected

questions.explanation: The ophthalmic (V1) division is the LEAST affected — the maxillary (V2) and mandibular (V3) divisions are most common, so that statement is false. Carbamazepine is the first-line drug. Flagged: the lacrimation/rhinorrhoea option is also atypical of classic TN, so more than one answer is arguable.

questions.stem: Which EARLY symptom presents with a hypopharyngeal tumour?
questions.explanation: Sore throat / throat discomfort is the typical early symptom (with dysphagia). Referred otalgia is also early/characteristic; dysphonia (laryngeal spread) and weight loss are late. Mild ambiguity between sore throat and referred otalgia.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a cause of chronic laryngitis?
questions.explanation: Hypothyroidism causes hoarseness via myxoedema/Reinke-type changes but is not classed as chronic laryngitis. LPR, smoking and vocal abuse are the classic causes.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a symptom of Ménière’s disease?
questions.explanation: Ménière’s tetrad = vertigo + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness. Headache is not part of it.
